quick_facts.label: What this exam is
quick_facts.value: “Ph.D. in Law Entrance Test” is a generic label for an institution-level doctoral admissions test, not one single all-India examination. Under the UGC Ph.D. Regulations, an HEI may admit through its own entrance test and/or through national-test fellowship/scholarship routes followed by interview.

quick_facts.label: Typical qualifying background
quick_facts.value: For Ph.D. admission, UGC regulations provide for a qualifying master’s degree with at least 55% aggregate marks or an equivalent grade, subject to specified category relaxations; universities may publish discipline-specific eligibility. NALSAR’s Law doctoral programme, for example, lists a relevant master’s degree in Law or cognate disciplines and 55% marks, with stated relaxations.

quick_facts.label: Test and interview rule
quick_facts.value: For an HEI-run entrance test, the UGC framework specifies 50% research methodology and 50% subject-specific syllabus. Candidates securing 50% in the test may be called for interview, with a 5% entrance-mark relaxation for specified categories; selection weightage is 70% test and 30% interview/viva voce.

quick_facts.label: Illustrative Law-school format
quick_facts.value: NALSAR’s published Ph.D. Law process illustrates the variation institutions can set: a 100-mark, 3.5-hour online examination with research aptitude/writing and disciplinary knowledge components, followed by a preliminary research proposal and interview stage. Its official page also states that UGC-NET/UGC-CSIR NET-qualified applicants or JRF holders are exempt from its written test.

quick_facts.label: Dates and fees vary by university
quick_facts.value: Applicants should use the particular university’s current notification rather than assume a common schedule or fee. For context only, NALSAR’s 2026–27 Ph.D. notice listed registration from June 12 to July 10, 2026 and an entrance examination on August 2, 2026; these dates are not a national schedule.

sections.heading: Purpose and place in legal education
paragraphs: This entrance test is a selection mechanism for admission to a university’s Ph.D. programme in Law after an LL.M. or another qualifying route accepted by that institution. It is not a nationally uniform examination: the UGC permits eligible higher-education institutions to use their own Ph.D. entrance test, while also permitting admission through qualifying national fellowship/scholarship tests followed by interview.

The test is designed to assess readiness for doctoral research rather than professional legal practice. The UGC framework requires an institution-run test to divide its syllabus equally between research methodology and subject-specific knowledge, which explains why Law Ph.D. admissions commonly assess both legal-domain understanding and research capability.
bullets: Read the university’s Ph.D. prospectus or admission notice as the controlling document for seats, discipline coverage, reservation, eligibility, test mode, exemptions, selection method, deadlines and fees.
Treat the test as one stage of doctoral selection: a research proposal, supervisor availability and interview can affect whether an applicant is ultimately admitted.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: At the UGC baseline, eligible applicants include candidates with a prescribed master’s-degree pathway and at least 55% aggregate marks or its equivalent grade. The regulations also provide a 5% marks/grade relaxation for SC, ST, OBC non-creamy-layer, Differently-Abled and EWS candidates, as determined by the Commission; they additionally recognize the specified four-year bachelor’s route and M.Phil. route. A university may impose relevant-subject or faculty-fit requirements in its own notification.

For Law-focused programmes, the immediate parent pathway is commonly an LL.M. NALSAR’s current doctoral programme page, for example, accepts a master’s degree or equivalent in Law, Humanities, Social Sciences or cognate disciplines with the stated minimum marks, and identifies certain professional qualifications as additional accepted routes. This is an example of institutional eligibility, not a rule that every university must copy.
bullets: Check whether your LL.M. specialization, marks, category certificate, NET/JRF status and proposed topic meet the specific institution’s published conditions.
Do not assume a national-test qualification automatically produces the same exemption everywhere: confirm the relevant university’s current notice.

sections.heading: Exam and selection format
paragraphs: Under the UGC Ph.D. Regulations, an HEI-administered entrance test has a 50:50 syllabus split between research methodology and subject-specific content. Applicants scoring 50% in that test are eligible to be called for interview, subject to the institution’s available seats and its published process. For selection based on that entrance test, the regulations prescribe 70% weightage for the test and 30% for interview/viva voce.

The exact question style, duration and delivery mode are institution-specific. NALSAR provides one Law-school example: its online test is stated to be 3.5 hours and 100 marks, split between research aptitude/writing skills and disciplinary knowledge. Its 2025–26 notification described the first component as including MCQs, essay, comprehension and précis writing, and the second as testing scholarly writing, referencing and domain knowledge.
bullets: Prepare for legal research methodology as well as substantive legal knowledge; neither component should be treated as optional.
Expect the interview to examine the proposed research problem, literature engagement, originality, feasibility and fit with available supervision, where those criteria are set by the institution.

sections.heading: Application process
paragraphs: The institution must publish its Ph.D. prospectus or notification in advance, including the available seats, subject/discipline-wise distribution, eligibility, admission procedure and other candidate information. Start by locating that official notice, then apply through the specified channel and retain proof of submission and payment.

A typical university-specific sequence is: verify eligibility and exemption status; submit the application and supporting academic/category documents; take the written test if required; submit a preliminary research proposal; and attend the interview if shortlisted. NALSAR’s Law notification is an official example: it required online application, provided a written-test exemption for specified UGC-NET/UGC-CSIR NET/JRF applicants, and required qualified or exempt applicants to submit a 1,500–2,000-word preliminary proposal for scrutiny before interview.
bullets: Use only the institution’s active admission portal and notification for deadlines. Dates, seats, application fees and test logistics change by university and admission cycle.
Before submitting a proposal, check faculty research areas, supervisor availability and already registered topics where the university makes these available.

Keep copies of degree/marks documents, identity documents, applicable category certificates, national-test certificates and the final submitted proposal; exact document requirements are university-specific.

sections.heading: Preparation strategy
paragraphs: Build preparation around the two UGC-prescribed content areas: research methodology and subject-specific knowledge. For a Law Ph.D. test, practise converting a broad legal interest into a researchable question, identifying relevant primary and secondary sources, explaining a defensible method, and using accurate legal citation and referencing.

Prepare the proposal and interview in parallel with the written test. NALSAR’s published process requires a preliminary proposal that states the research problem and literature survey and demonstrates an unexplored topic capable of an original scholarly contribution. Its notice also states that proposals are screened for duplication and plagiarism, and that internal supervisory expertise is a factor in consideration.
bullets: Review research design, doctrinal and empirical approaches, research ethics, literature-review methods, legal citation and academic writing.
Revise the legal field closest to your proposal and practise timed analytical writing, not only objective questions.
Draft a concise proposal early; be ready to explain its question, gap, sources, method, feasibility and likely contribution in an interview.
Independently check citations, quotations and paraphrasing before submission; do not reuse another person’s work or submit a topic already registered at the institution.

sections.heading: What follows after the test
paragraphs: Passing an entrance test does not by itself guarantee Ph.D. admission. Under the UGC framework, test performance determines eligibility to be called for interview, and the number called may depend on seats. At NALSAR, applicants whose proposal clears the institution’s scrutiny and who qualify in interview are provisionally admitted, illustrating that written performance, proposal quality and interview are distinct stages.

Admission begins the doctoral programme rather than ending the selection process. UGC regulations set a minimum Ph.D. duration of three years including coursework, with a normal maximum of six years from admission, subject to the regulations’ re-registration and category-related provisions. NALSAR’s Law notice also states that admitted candidates undertake foundational research-methodology coursework and further coursework before defending a modified proposal.
bullets: The immediate outcome of a successful process is admission to a supervised Ph.D. in Law programme, subject to the institution’s conditions and seat/supervisor capacity.
Confirm the university’s current coursework, residency, fee and thesis requirements after admission; these are programme-specific and can change between academic years.
